summary:
The Komsa culture was one of the earliest Mesolithic hunter-gatherer traditions of Arctic Norway and the far northern Scandinavian coast, dated to approximately 10,000–6,000 BCE. Named after a mountain in Finnmark County, northern Norway, the culture represents the first known human colonization of the High Arctic coastal zone of Scandinavia following the retreat of the Fennoscandian ice sheet. Komsa communities migrated northward along the Norwegian coast as glaciers retreated, following the expanding coastal and fjord environments rich in marine resources.

Komsa communities were primarily coastal and maritime foragers, exploiting the rich cold-water resources of the Norwegian coast including seals, walrus, sea birds, fish, and shellfish. Their sites are found on raised beaches and terraces now well above sea level — the land having rebounded after the weight of the glaciers was removed, a process known as isostatic rebound. The Komsa tool industry includes large, somewhat crude-looking stone tools adapted for a maritime environment, including large core-and-flake artifacts suited for processing sea mammals. The Komsa culture is part of a broader pattern of rapid human colonization of the newly deglaciated coasts of northern Europe during the early Holocene, a process that also included the [[event:1279|Mesolithic colonization of Scandinavia]] more broadly.
